data.title: Apple Gains From Trump Tariff Reversal, But Is Still Too Pricey
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) stock got a major dose of good luck Friday, as the U.S. government announced that computers and smartphones had been exempted from Donald Trump's China tariffs. Prior to this, the stock was undergoing a severe selloff, as the China tariffs were perceived as having a severe impact on it. Indeed, with 90% of iPhone manufacturing taking place in China, the tariffs probably would have had an impact, had they not been reversed.

It appears likely that Apple stock will experience a rally on Monday because of this news. The stock had actually been holding its own relatively well in the earlier months of Trump’s administration; it was when Trump’s tariffs on China started rising dramatically that it really took a beating.

That’s where we stand today. Down 24% for the year when the NASDQ-100 is down 13%, AAPL is underperforming the index that it is a part of. That is an unusual occurrence. Apple has long been one of the most stable and dependable NASDAQ stocks, owing to its high margins, strong brand identity, and high (historical) growth. However, the company has largely ceased growing, with revenue up 2.6% and earnings down 2% over the trailing 12-month period.

So the situation for Apple is not as fortuitous as it was a few years ago, when its stock was rising rapidly in the market.

If you look at the Apple rating next to this article immediately after reading the text up to this point, you might find the rating confusing. I’ve said that AAPL is likely to rally on Monday; based on this, shouldn’t I think the stock a buy? There are several reasons why I don’t think the likely Monday rally is material to investors:

First, it isn’t feasible for most investors to buy stocks over the weekend through normal channels. AAPL stock will likely open higher in Sunday futures and on the NASDAQ on Monday more or less instantly, meaning only high frequency traders will be able to take advantage of the bounce at open.

Second, a person buying the stock after a bounce occurs, may be paying too high of a price. As I wrote above, Apple had almost no revenue growth and negative earnings growth in the most recent 12-month (TTM) period. Despite this, AAPL trades at 28 times earnings, 7.6 times sales and 44.6 times book value. That is a steep valuation for a company that is not growing. This does not contradict my prediction that AAPL stock will rally on Monday, as investors clearly see it as being worth 28 times earnings--I just disagree with their assessment. Going by the logic that Apple was worth $198 at Friday’s close, it should be worth $198 + X when the NASDAQ opens on Monday, with tariff relief part of the fact set.

When I last covered Apple, I rated the stock a hold on the grounds that it was trading at a nosebleed valuation while having no growth catalysts. I maintain the same rating today; however, since the stock's price now is lower than it was when I last wrote about it, I should explain why I still think it’s a hold at this valuation. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will do that.

Trump’s Tariff Reversal

Before going any further, I should explain what Trump’s about-face on tariffs will likely do for Apple, what it won’t do, and why the stock is likely to have a brief reaction to the move on Monday.

Trump’s tariff reversal will prevent a scenario in which Apple’s hardware becomes 145% more expensive than it was before the tariffs were implemented. That is undoubtedly good news for the company—I mean, good news compared to the situation on Friday, when it looked like the tariffs would be paid.

An entry-level iPhone 16 is listed at $799 on Apple’s website. Unless Apple were to eat some of the cost, a 145% tariff would raise that price to $1,958. Given that customers had been used to $799 entry-level iPhones prior to April 2, a price hike to $1,958 would likely have either depressed revenue (if Apple ate none of the cost), or increased costs. Either way, EBIT and net income would have taken a hit.

Donald Trump’s Friday tariff reversal removes that scenario from the list of apparent outcomes. Those who thought Apple worth $198 on Friday, will likely think it worth more on Monday. So I expect investors to buy Apple stock on Monday. I’m not, however, convinced that the gains will last, for reasons I’ll outline below.

Minimal Growth Prospects

I wrote earlier that Apple’s trailing 12-month growth was lackluster, despite the company’s stock trading at a valuation that needs growth to be sustained. The company’s future growth prospects are also limited, for several reasons.

First, there is the simple fact that Apple is so massive in scale. The company does nearly $400 billion in annual revenue, and 24% of that is pure profit. Finding profitable opportunities at that kind of scale isn’t easy. Simply sinking more money into advertising or producing more of Apple’s existing offerings would likely push the company to the point of diminishing marginal returns. Breaking into new markets, on the other hand, would be difficult, as there are few markets that move the needle for a $400 billion per year business.

We can see the difficulty of finding “needle-moving” businesses for Apple by looking at its recent efforts at doing so.

The first of those was the Vision Pro. This was Apple’s big leap into virtual reality (VR). The product took several years and billions of dollars to develop. Sales ultimately disappointed. Supply chain data indicates that Apple is no longer actively manufacturing the product, although it remains listed on the company's website.

Another was the Apple Car. This never saw the light of day, although Apple spent about a decade and billions of dollars working on it. The project was cancelled in February 2024.

The fact that neither of the above projects got particularly far shows the difficulty of finding meaningful investment opportunities at Apple’s scale.

So, Apple’s avenues to future growth are limited. Investing more money into its current operations would probably lead to diminishing returns, while successful new opportunities are proving hard to find.

Valuation

With the problem with Apple’s lack of growth opportunities is the fact that the company’s stock has a steep valuation. First, let’s look at the multiples that Seeking Alpha Quant has on file.

Apple trades at 28.4 times earnings, 7.6. times sales, 44.6 times book and 27.5 times cash flow. These are growth stock multiples, yet Apple has limited growth prospects. This suggests that the company won't pay investors back--in dividends, buybacks or anything else--in a reasonable timeframe. It’s also worth noting that most of Apple’s multiples are higher than those of its sector, and the IT sector is generally a pricey one.

Next, we can look at Apple’s discounted cash flows. Because of Apple’s limited growth prospects, we can evaluate these with no assumed growth.

In the trailing 12-month period, Apple had $6.45 in FCF per share. Discounted at 4.5% (the current 10-year treasury yield), that’s worth $143. Discounted at 10%, it’s worth $64.50. These figures are both well below Apple’s current price. So, I would not buy Apple stock today.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Apple is that it has a near term catalyst, but is still too pricey. It’s quite likely that Friday’s tariff relief will lead to a bounce on Monday, but the bounce will likely be short-lived. Apple has a great brand and a wide moat, but limited growth opportunities. The most I’d pay for it is about $150.

data.title: Alphabet: The Cheapest Of The Mag 7 And The Least Exposed To Tariffs
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG, GOOGL) has traditionally been one of the most reasonably priced stocks among the Mag 7. Following the broader market selloff that tanked pretty much every US-based equity in the tech sector, Alphabet now looks more compelling than ever.

In fact, among the Mag 7, Google is the least affected by the steep 145% tariff on Chinese goods. That’s mainly because most of its business is based on services, not physical products.

How about Google's Pixel smartphone? - some may ask.

Well, it turns out that the US has introduced (yet another) exemption on tariffs, including smartphones and other electronics components supplied largely by China.

This is great news for the market. In fact, I foresee a V shape recovery over the next 90 days.

As a side note, my portfolio is fully invested at the moment of writing this article, although I don't own Alphabet shares. I own Nvidia and Broadcom (after panic buying them on April 9), although I am considering a full-send move (what's diversification, anyways?) next week in the SP5Y, a 5x leveraged ETF on the S&P 500.

Back to my thesis on Google, there are risks to consider, including the volatile trade policy of the new administration and its implication on inflation and hyperscaler CapEx.

Google - A Safe Haven Amidst Tariffs

Let’s start with a quick look at the history, then, we’ll move on to the numbers.

Google entered the Chinese market in 2006 with a censored version of its search engine (google.cn), reaching 36% market share in just three years. However, due to several cyberattacks and Beijing's stringent censorship rules, Google redirected Chinese users to its Hong Kong site instead. Ultimately, this decision led China's Great Firewall to ban Google's search engine in 2010.

In 2018, Google attempted to reenter the Chinese market through Project Dragonfly, a censored search engine compliant with China's stringent internet regulations. This project faced an internal pushback when Google employees signed a letter raising concerns about its ethics. By July 2019, Google officially ended Project Drangonfly.

Therefore, both Google Services and Google Cloud are safe from tariffs. Not just because these are not available in China but because these business segments are categorized as services and not goods. So far, the tariff drama between the US and China is targeting physical goods only. That said, if tensions keep rising between the US and China, I wouldn’t be surprised if services end up being targeted too.

How about Google's Pixel? Aren't they manufactured in China?

Yes and no. Let me explain.

Back in 2016, Google chose China as its primary manufacturing hub for its new Pixel smartphone. However, in 2019, amidst escalating trade tensions between the US and China during Trump's first administration, Google began relocating a portion of its Pixel smartphone manufacturing to Vietnam. More recently, in 2024, Google further diversified its manufacturing footprint by commencing the assembly of Pixel devices in India. In fact, Dixon Technologies (contract manufacturer of Pixel's and some of Apple's mobile devices) more than doubled its revenue last year, which is a clear sign of how US based companies are diversifying their contract manufacturers away from China.

Unfortunately, Google does not break down Pixel sales in its financial reporting. All we have is a mention in the 10-K, stating that "devices, which primarily include sales of the Pixel family" are categorized within the Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices subsegment within Google Services.

Despite not knowing the percentage of Pixel units manufactured in China and imported into the US, I argue that this figure is irrelevant. Why?

This Friday afternoon, approximately one hour before the end of the trading session, the US announced that a list of tech products, including smartphones, laptop computers, and chip making equipment, will be exempt from the reciprocal tariffs. So, the 145% tariff on imports from China doesn't apply to Google's Pixel phones (or any smartphones).

This, combined with the fact that the tariffs only apply to goods and not services, keeps Google’s earnings largely untouched. At least during the next 90 days.

ChatGPT Isn’t Replacing Google Search Anytime Soon

When the AI boom took off in mid-2023, one of my biggest worries was that Google Search might soon become useless. However, Alphabet's FY 2024 results are clear proof that search is still going strong and continues to be a key part of their business.

Take a look at the table below, particularly in the % change in revenue for google and youtube ads.

Do you see the double digit revenue increase in search and YouTube ads? Well, that’s clear proof for skeptics (like me, not too long ago) that Google Search isn’t becoming obsolete because of AI. Just look at the top keywords by PPC, and you will quickly realize that people don't use AI (at the moment) to search for these services. When you're looking for a business or a product, Google Search is the go-to platform. I don't see this changing anytime soon. In fact, Google is now using Gemini for AI overviews on search queries, reaching more than 1 billion users across 100 countries, according to the latest 10-K.

Furthermore, Google's Gemini is advancing towards its goal of reaching 500 million users by the end of 2025. That said, even if they reach this target, they would still be lagging behind ChatGPT, which has approximately 400 million weekly users. So far, Alphabet's latest LLM developments include Gemini 2.5 Flash, a streamlined version of the Gemini 2.5 Pro that allows developers to adjust processing time based on query complexity. On top of that, Deep Research has now become available to Gemini Advanced subscribers utilizing the Gemini 2.5 Pro Experimental model.

Valuation

I am about to prove that Alphabet is the cheapest and least exposed company to tariffs among the Mag 7.

I considered building the table below, showing a valuation comparison by P/E, P/S, P/CF, and P/FCF (all TTM values, as I don't trust the Street's estimates for next year). Additionally, I considered adding a column showing CapEx.

On a P/E basis, Alphabet is trading at just 18x last year's earnings. That's the cheapest ratio among the entire Mag 7 group. On a P/S basis, Alphabet is the second cheapest after Amazon. This makes sense, considering Amazon's massive online retail business. As for me, I prefer earnings over just sales.

On a price to cash flow basis (both OCF and FCF), Alphabet is nearly tied with META. Both companies look quite attractive on this metric. If I had to pick between Meta and Alphabet, I’d go with Alphabet since it looks more attractive on a P/S and P/E basis.

Naturally, the broader market selloff has brought Alphabet's valuation (P/E, P/CF) significantly below its 5-year median.

Overall, I see a company undervalued among its peers and its own historical performance.

Risks

Despite the tariff drama that we have experienced over the past 6-8 weeks, Google has reaffirmed its $75 billion CapEx spending plan for this year. Additionally, as I said before, the tariffs are not applicable to chip making equipment. Therefore, I don't forsee an increase in GPU costs, at least, not within the next 90 days. Given how unpredictable US trade policy has been over the past weeks, no one knows what will happen after the 90 day pause. This is a risk to consider. In fact, this is the exact reason why my bull thesis has a timeframe of 90 days.

The biggest risk I see now is (yet) another plot twist, where the new administration changes its mind again and goes ahead with semiconductor tariffs. If that's the case, I believe we could see a period of high inflation in the US, close to 2022 levels (only that this time, inflation would be driven by one man, not by an overly stimulated economy).

If semiconductor tariffs kick in, I think Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Google will scale back their CapEx spending this year. That could trigger a selloff in the tech sector since the skyhigh valuations (just look at Palantir's 400 P/E ratio) have been supported by the AI narrative. If enterprises and consumers don’t adopt AI as quickly as the market expects, there could be more trouble ahead.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I see Alphabet as one of the most undervalued stocks among the Mag 7, with the least exposure to tariffs due to the nature of its business. Current trade policies with China don't affect services like Google Search and Cloud, as Google is not operating in this region. Additionally, Pixel phones manufactured in China are exempt from the new tariffs, while the company is diversifying manufacturing outside of China.

Furthermore, Google Search is not becoming obsolete due to AI, as noted by the double digit revenue growth in FY 2024. Therefore, I rate Google as a strong buy, with a timeframe of approximately 90 days in mind. Risks include deterioration in the current trade policy due to the unpredictable decisions of the current administration.

data.title: PCMM: Why This Private Credit ETF Deserves A Spot On Your Watchlist
data.description:
For investors feeling spooked about the volatility found in equities, one might find some relative stability in the credit market. We now have more choices than ever for these high-quality ETFs, and this article will explore one that has recently found its way on my radar.

Overview

The BondBloxx Private Credit CLO ETF (NASDAQ:PCMM) offers investors a way to access the private credit collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) market, which was traditionally reserved for institutional and ultra-high-net-worth investors. This fund is actively managed and focuses on CLOs made to mid-sized companies. Specifically, PCMM targets firms with an annual EBITDA between $10 million and $250 million. PCMM is brand new, having launched in December 2024.

The ETF is actively managed and has an expense ratio of 0.68%, along with a yield to maturity of 8.31%. PCMM pays a monthly dividend and has an annual yield of 1.82%. Thus, for those looking for pure income opportunities, PCMM might deserve a pass. However, for those seeking capital preservation as well as diversification from traditional bonds and public credit markets, PCMM adequately fills this niche use case. With PCMM allowing one to access private credit without locking up capital in private funds, it reduces the barrier of entry significantly. It has $107.62 million worth of assets under management.

Portfolio Structure

PCMM's portfolio structure is unique in that at least 80% of its assets must be allocated to private credit CLO tranches. PCMM also attempts to maintain an average credit rating of "A" and avoids equity tranches altogether.

At the time of writing it holds 54 CLO securities, with most of them being senior class A notes that have credit ratings that range from AAA to A, with some minor additional allocations to BBB and BB tranches.

PCMM has 96.6% of its securities in the United States, and it has a notable concentration in its top 10 holdings, with these assets accounting for 52% of its total portfolio. While this may seem concentrated at first glance, CLOs generally comprise hundreds of loans, which gives PCMM a surprisingly resilient portfolio structure.

In addition to having the sheer breadth of loans, PCMM also invests in a range of industries, with Software being the dominant sector.

Name % of Net Assets
Software 11.40%
Health Care Providers & Services 7.90%
Commercial Services & Supplies 5.40%
Professional Services 5.30%
Insurance 5.20%
Diversified Consumer Services 3.40%
Health Care Technology 3.20%
IT Services 2.80%
Health Care Equipment & Supplies 2.30%
Containers & Packaging 1.90%
Other 51.00%

One thing that investors may appreciate about PCMM's portfolio is that the fund's duration is near-zero at 0.06 years, as all of its holdings are pegged to a floating-rate. This means that it is insulated from interest rate risks.

Outlook

My outlook for the private credit market is positive, especially for CLOs. U.S. volumes for CLOs surged, hitting $202 billion, with middle-market CLOs making up $38 billion of that total figure.

My view is that CLOs will continue to perform strongly this year as well as into 2026. The reason is that the consensus is that the Fed is expected to keep interest rates elevated in order to curb inflation. According to the Fed, tariffs are a key reason why it could lead to an inflationary environment.

Furthermore, despite the higher rates, default rates are generally low, especially among middle-market loans that PCMM specializes in. In the direct lending market as a whole, the default rate stands at 1.9%, which is 1% below the long-term average.

Finally, it's also key to remember that middle-market CLOs are relatively new, having only gained serious attention post-2015. The explosive growth of private credit led to this growth, and I believe that institutional investors will continue to pour money into them, as many have attractive risk-adjusted risk profiles, which is ideal for pension funds, insurance companies, and asset managers to hold for the long run.

Peer Analysis

Perhaps due to being released very recently, PCMM is in an unfavorable position on a net yield basis when one compares it against its peers in the table below. For those seeking high income, a mix of JAAA and JBBB or CLOZ could lead to a combined dividend yield in the high 6% range. This could successfully balance the need for high yield as well as credit and market risks.

ETF Issuer Credit Focus Risk Level Expense Ratio Dividend Yield Net Yield Notes
PCMM BondBloxx Private Credit CLOs (Middle Market) Moderate–High 0.68% 1.82% 1.14%

Focused on newer, less liquid private credit CLOs
JAAA Janus Henderson AAA-rated CLO tranches Low 0.21% 6.13% 5.92%

Conservative, high-quality CLO exposure
JBBB Janus Henderson B to BBB-rated CLO tranches Moderate 0.49% 7.85% 7.36%

Targets lower investment grade for more income
BCLO BlackRock (iShares) BBB to B- CLO tranches Moderate 0.45% 1.30% 0.85%

Yield-focused with a big asset manager behind it
CLOZ Panagram BBB to B CLO tranches Moderate–High 0.50% 8.89% 8.39%

Newer ETF, high-yield exposure to riskier CLO segments

PCMM is also unattractive due to having the highest expense ratio, while also offering the lowest dividend yield.

However, it's important to note that since PCMM is brand-new, its trailing dividend yield is misleading, since it's only accounting for three of the monthly dividends it has paid out. When using the 30-day SEC yield, PCMM's performance is actually above average, as one can see in the table below. The caveat is that it's better than what it appears at first glance, but when one takes its higher expense ratio into consideration, it's a little bit on the pricier side without an edge that has materialized yet.

Ticker

30-Day SEC Yield
PCMM 7.29%
JAA 5.45%
JBBB 7.54%
BCLO 7.82%

Finally, these ETFs are not immune to market stresses, as they still have at least some correlation with equities. For instance, the more speculative ETFs like JBBB sold off hard during the market panic of Trump's tariffs, but JAAA's credit quality insulated it from dropping even further.

Risks

A key risk for PCMM is that it invests in private credit CLOs, which are less liquid than those that are traded publicly. With less volume naturally comes wider spreads and reduced flexibility for buying and selling the securities. What this means in practice is that these spreads could lead to an erosion in the fund's NAV during times of high market stress.

The other important risk I see is that it has a very limited operating history, and although its SEC yield is competitive, we have yet to see it materialize in the form of dividends and returns for shareholders. PCMM is competing against some very well-known and trusted ETFs like JAAA or JBBB with longer operating histories, which makes it comparably more risky in this regard.

Conclusion

I rate PCMM as a Hold, and it is an ETF for income investors to keep on their watch lists. I'd like to see the expense ratio lower as it accumulates more assets under management, and for it to prove that it has a clear edge over its peers before making a Buy recommendation. It has all the pieces to potentially offer better risk-adjusted returns through its portfolio, but some confirmation is required to validate this hypothesis.

data.title: NATO: Tariff War Helps Raise Defense Stocks Over The Market
data.description:
As President Donald J. Trump emerged victorious in the 2024 election and prepared to begin his second term, his team informed European officials that he expects their respective countries to spend at least 5% of their Gross Domestic Product (GDP) on building military capabilities.

As of 2024, it was estimated that virtually every country - even the U.S. - fell short of this target.

However, it bears noting that the U.S.' spending is significantly higher than that of almost every one of its European allies combined. Additionally, given the significant deployment of U.S. military assets over in Europe to buttress its defense from threats, it is intuitively logical that Europe could ideally stand to be more invested in its own defense (and an idea supported by many a U.S. think tank as well).

In the wake of President Trump's re-election, plans were underway within the European Union (E.U.) to raise spending by €500 billion per year, which the European Commissioner for Defense Industry and Space Andrius Kubilius argued was only enough to cover an air defense program. Also, many of the bloc's nations balked at the prospect of raising the annual target from 2024's 2% to a mere 3% (which would cost the E.U. around €200 billion), due to economic difficulties and already-stretched balance sheets. Poland, an already-aggressive spender owing to its proximity to the ongoing Russo-Ukrainian conflict, suggested one of two methods to raise the required capital: the E.U. could sell more joint bonds backed by its own long-term budget or create a new special purpose vehicle modelled on the eurozone bailout fund, the European Stability Mechanism, to provide loans by selling bonds backed by paid-in and callable capital from participating countries.

The first option found few takers but the potential for non-E.U. countries such as the United Kingdom and Norway to participate in the second option created some buzz; however, markets expect that this "new borrower" would have pay a premium over the interest the E.U. pays for its bonds - thus adding to the cost burden.

In light of these difficulties, President Trump's demand for spending to go up by an ever larger margin had found few enthusiastic takers. On the 6th of March this year, the European Commission proposed the "ReArm Europe" plan, which aimed to raise €650 billion over four years (i.e. an average of €175 billion each year) by allowing member nations to increase their military budgets to up to 1.5% of GDP without being counted in their national deficits and raising €150 billion through EU-issued bonds, which would then be lent to member states at low-interest rates and long repayment terms.

The plan almost instantly ran into trouble, with Italy and Spain demanding broader definition of the plan to include anti-terrorism, satellite communication, artificial intelligence and quantum computing capabilities. Both nations also expressed discomfort with the name, with Spain stating outright that Southern Europe faces threats that are altogether different from those faced by Eastern Europe. The plan's name was amended to "Readiness 2030".

Steering Away from U.S. Dependence?

E.U. officials proposed that European countries can partake in the €150 billion loan program - titled "SAFE" (Security Action for Europe) - if the products being procured have a definitive Europe connection. For ammunition, missiles and small drones, 65% of the costs must originate inside the E.U., European Economic Area (EEA), European Free Trade Association (EFTA) countries or Ukraine, with the manufacturer being located within these blocs and not controlled by another country. For more complex systems such as air and missile defense systems and larger drones, it must be possible to substitute components that could be subject to restrictions imposed by other countries.

While it is possible for an E.U.-based subsidiary of a British or U.S. company, the cost origination aspect implies that weapons manufactured in a British or American plant might not be eligible - a move that rankled U.S. officials, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio reportedly warning Europe that any exclusion of U.S. companies would be seen negatively by Washington. However, in light of the ongoing tariff war, some degree of market access limitation might be imposed on U.S. manufacturers.

This warning has a certain logic: outside of Ukraine (which became the world’s largest importer of major arms in the 2020–24 period), arms imports by the E.U. more than doubled between 2015–19 and 2020–24, with the U.S. supplying 64% of these arms.

With arms supplied to 107 countries in 2020-24, U.S. arm exports increased by 21% between 2015–19 and 2020–24, with its share of global arms exports growing from 35% to 43%. In effect, the U.S. is resolutely the world's largest arms exporter - with France a very distant second.

For the first time in two decades, the largest share of U.S. arms exports in 2020–24 went to Europe (35%) rather than the Middle East (33%), thus possibly underlining Secretary Rubio's concerns.

Despite calls from within Europe to limit dependence on U.S. imports, European NATO members have over 500 combat aircraft and many other complex weapons systems still on order from U.S. suppliers. However, with "smaller" weapons and systems, it is likely that European manufacturers might be net beneficiaries.

Market Impact

While it is by now common knowledge that the broad market S&P 500 (SPX in index form; SPY in ETF form) has been under significant pressure and volatility in the lead-up to President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement, defense stocks have shown a widely varying trajectory as driven by investor convictions:

Despite both being European companies that are potentially beneficiaries of the E.U.'s largesse for arms buying, German arms manufacturer Rheinmetall AG (OTCPK:RNMBF; OTCPK:RNMBY) substantially outperforms relative to France's engine manufacturer Safran S.A. (OTCPK:SAFRF; OTCPK:SAFRY). Despite being in the same industry as Safran (i.e. aviation systems) while potentially impacted by E.U. limitations, U.S. aviation technology majors Northrop Grumman (NOC) and Lockheed Martin (LMT) have been somewhat resurgent.

Rheinmetall is expected to be a wide-ranging beneficiary from increases in E.U.'s expenditure on projectile weapon systems and munitions while historical "big-ticket" buy-ins of aviation systems by the likes of Northrop Grumman and Lockheed Martin by European armies are unlikely to be substituted out before said systems' end of life. Also, given the top-of-the-line performance characteristics by these systems, it's unlikely that European armies would be willing to settle for less or not be receptive to next-generation products. This creates some bottlenecks for the likes of Safran as they're found predominantly in French/European-produced aircraft.

Rheinmetall, Northrop Grumman and Lockheed Martin also outperform the broad market by a comfortable margin. Meanwhile, the Themes Transatlantic Defense ETF (NASDAQ:NATO) - which includes all four companies along many other manufacturers - pulls at nearly 10% outperformance relative to the S&P 500 as measured from the end of February till the 11th of April. After a period of decline, the "NATO" ETF is now drawing flat relative to the end of February and has a slight bullish momentum being imputed.

A "Balanced" Investing Style in the Sector?

The "NATO", Themes Transatlantic Defense ETF is a more recent entrant in the defense ETFs space, where the likes of the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA) and the Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA) are likely to be better known to investors. Both of the latter solely feature U.S. tickers, with the top 10 holdings being strikingly similar, albeit different in terms of weight distribution.

The "NATO" ETF, on the other hand (and as the name suggests), is a little more international:

While the top 10 holdings for the "NATO" ETF is closer in terms of weight distribution to that of Invesco's "PPA" ETF, it features the solid outperformer Rheinmetall in the top position. Also, the Top 10 holdings have a near-even balance between U.S. and E.U. tickers.

In overall holdings, however, U.S. tickers do make up almost 60% of the ETF by weight. But with the remainder being driven by tickers from the Continent, it has a profile that is distinct from both the U.S. ETFs.

An interesting feature is that the ETF assigns a slightly higher average constituent weight to European (i.e. EU, EEA and EFTA) tickers than it does to tickers from other regions, including the U.S.

In Conclusion

The favorable representation of European tickers in the "NATO" ETF might prove to be an interesting feature that might enable investors to capture the uptrend in European military spending. At the same time, it is unlikely that European nations would commit vast sums of capital to develop alternative "high-ticket" weapon systems that are entirely distinct or free from U.S. suppliers.

Overall, regardless of economic conditions, it is increasingly likely that nations in the Western Hemisphere remain locked into sustained and increased military spending, potentially creating ample opportunities for outperformance versus the market. The "NATO" ETF's distribution of weights across regions adds an interesting layer of consideration for investors looking to capitalize on spending patterns being implied by Western governments. It might make sense for investors to consider diversifying their portfolio by including this relatively recent instrument.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Profit Engine: A Financial Anatomy Of Union Pacific
data.description:
Introduction

Union Pacific (NYSE:UNP) today is the most powerful transportation artery in the western United States: more than 32,000 miles of tracks, 8,300 locomotives, and about 10,000 customers. UP connects the Pacific Coast, Mexico and the industrial heart of America — the Midwest. And, importantly, it is the only company in the country with access to all six key crossings on the border with Mexico — a real advantage in an era when logistics is increasingly becoming a matter of geopolitics.

Recent years have been difficult but important for Union Pacific. The transition to the Exact schedule model (PSR) proved to be painful, especially against the background of the staffing shortage in 2022. With the arrival of Jim Vena in 2023, the strategy was reversed: from cost reduction, the focus has shifted to stability, reliability and growth. Already in 2024, this gave a result — the level of security on the network increased significantly, and the volume of international containers in the second half of the year soared by 30% compared to the same period in 2023. At the same time, the company is upgrading its technological infrastructure. The modernization of all major operating systems, including the introduction of NetControl, has been completed. In 2024, Union Pacific emerged from protracted turbulence with a flexible, technological business.

Business model

Union Pacific is a huge logistics machine built into the economy of the whole country. They earn money by moving goods through their colossal network, which stretches for more than 32 thousand miles.

Their clients range from farmers and energy companies to retailers and chemists, that is, the entire spectrum of those who need to transport large and heavy goods. The cost of the trip depends on the weight, distance, type of cargo, and even the cost of fuel.

They divide the entire business into three categories: Bulk is a bulk like coal, grain and fertilizers; Industrial is everything industrial: chemicals, building materials, oil, metals; and Premium is the top segment where containers and cars are spinning. It is Premium that generates the most margin, especially given the revival of global trade. Their entire network operates end-to-end: routes are centralized from the headquarters in Omaha, cargo is safely transferred between railways and regional lines, and the entire infrastructure from terminals to tracks is used as flexibly as possible.

The year 2024 has just shown how the company is able to adapt. Bulk sank — the winter was mild, gas was cheap, and coal went down. Grain and fertilizers gained a little, but the segment as a whole passed. At Industrial, volumes dropped slightly, but prices went up, and this saved revenue. The transportation of oil and chemicals was particularly impressive. But Premium has become a real driver. After a rough start to the year, the second half showed strong growth — more than 30% year-on-year for intermodal transport, when cargo was reoriented from the East and Canada back to the West. And even with cars, everything was not bad — despite the downtime at the factories, new contracts allowed us to keep volumes.

Union Pacific in general has transformed a lot in recent years. Previously, it lived mainly on coal and grain, but now it relies on marginal segments such as containers, automobiles, and industry. The PSR model has improved productivity: fewer locomotives, fewer crews, but more cargo per train. But at the same time, the company has kept the capacity in reserve so as not to lose flexibility. This helps to cope with peak loads and not to drop the service. Plus, they are actively integrating with autologistics, digital services for customers, big data and green technologies.

Competitors

Rail freight transportation in the USA is a mature market, where six large Class I operators have long divided the entire map among themselves. And in this field, Union Pacific is one of the key players in the West. Together with BNSF, they actually control the entire backbone logistics from the Pacific coast to Chicago. UP interacts more with eastern players like CSX Corporation (CSX) and Norfolk Southern (NSC) through connections, providing transcontinental routes. In 2023, another player appeared on the arena — CPKC (CP), formed after the merger of Canadian Pacific and Kansas City Southern, and it was he who intensified the north-south competition, especially in the Mexican direction. But even so, the main challenge for UP is not other railways at all, but motor transport, which, due to its flexibility, still picks up a significant part of the cargo, especially in the range of 500-1500 miles.

From the point of view of macroeconomics, 2024 passed under the flag of stagnation. The entire industry showed an increase of only 0.3%, and the drivers were not traditional coal or construction goods, but the recovery of the automotive industry and the growth of intermodal transportation. As for the struggle within the railway industry, UP's main competitor is still BNSF. They run parallel on many routes, especially from West Coast ports to Chicago. Yes, BNSF has long had a reputation as an operator with a more flexible service, but over the past two years, Union Pacific has really improved. Their metrics for speed, turnover of wagons, and accuracy of delivery have improved, and customers are starting to notice this. Cooperation with eastern roads is based on the principle of "together against others" — they cooperate at junctions in order to maintain competitiveness against trucks. But with CPKC, the situation is getting worse — it is no longer a partner, but a full-fledged rival in the southern direction.

But the toughest competition is still against trucking. And that's where UP got a good window of opportunity in 2024. High fuel prices, a shortage of drivers, and increased customer ESG campaigns are playing into the hands of the railways. UP actively promotes the topic of ecology — CO₂ emissions are 75% lower compared to trucks. But in order for customers to actually transfer to the rails, ESG alone is not enough — it is necessary that the cargo arrives on time. After severe criticism in 2022, Union Pacific worked on the mistakes: expanded the pool of machinists, introduced a reserve of locomotives, updated processes — and in 2024 the network became noticeably more flexible. These improvements have really helped to take back some of the intermodal traffic, especially in the second half of the year.

The final scenario at the entrance to 2025 is as follows: the market is growing slowly, there are enough challenges, but Union Pacific is holding on confidently. Plus the infrastructure, reach, and customer base — all this provides a powerful foundation to hold positions and aim to increase the share where it really works.

Financials

After the disastrous 2020, when the pandemic hit the entire industry sharply, UP quickly regained its position: in 2021, revenue grew at double-digit rates. In 2024, Union Pacific continued to grow, increasing operating revenue to $24.25 billion— up 1% year-on-year. Volumes went up by 2%, and tariffs went up too, although the drop in diesel prices slowed down the final result a little.

I expect revenue growth in the range of 2-3% in 2025, to $24.8–25.0 billion. This is a moderate but realistic scenario: a small plus in terms of volumes (coal and intermodal), plus a moderate indexation of tariffs. I expect that UP will partially shift inflation to customers and improve the structure of the cargo portfolio. Intermodal will drop slightly in the first half of the year due to the high base, but the trend should level off in the second half of the year. Bulk may increase against the background of stable gas prices, Industrial will be stable, and Premium is the main candidate for growth. On the horizon of 2026-2027, I see an acceleration to 3-4% per year and a potential revenue of over $27 billion. This does not look like an ambitious over—plan; rather, it is a realistic path against the background of soft economic growth and the continued flow of goods to the railway.

In recent years, Union Pacific has been steadily increasing margins, and 2024 proved this: the company generated $12.1 billion in EBITDA with a margin of about 50%, and net profit increased to $6.747 billion, which is almost 28% of net margin. Operating profit increased by 7% to $9.7 billion. All this despite the fact that revenue increased by only 1% — there is a competent cost management.

I conservatively expect that by 2027, the EBITDA margin will remain at the current level in the range of 49-50%, and the absolute EBITDA will reach $13-14 billion. Due to digitalization, automation, and new technology, costs should grow more slowly than inflation. An important point is that even with rising salaries, UP keeps spending growth at 3-4% per year. If fuel prices remain moderate and the company continues to upgrade its fleet of locomotives, the savings will be consolidated in the model. I would also like to note a reduction in interest expenses to $1.269 billion and an effective tax rate of around 24%. All this will allow the company to gradually achieve a net profit above $7.5 billion by 2027 with a margin of about 28%.

The financial position of Union Pacific at the end of 2024 looks stable and confident. The company's total debt has decreased to $31.2 billion, and the Debt/EBITDA indicator remains at a comfortable level of about 2.67x, which is quite acceptable for capital-intensive infrastructure. At the same time, interest payments do not put pressure on business coverage at the level of 7.93x. Importantly, the company does not just service debt, but uses excess free cash flow to reduce it: in 2024, UP repaid debts of $2.2 billion, while raising only $800 million in new debt. Net debt has decreased, and the debt structure remains predominantly fixed, with low sensitivity to rates.

Capital expenditures in 2024 amounted to $3.45 billion, or about 14% of total revenue. This is a typical level for a railway (not wasteful or undervalued). For example, part of the capital went to the early purchase of leased locomotives (advantageous at high rental rates). In the future, I expect Capex to grow slightly at face value, but at about a stable 13-14% of revenue, which coincides with the forecast of management. In other words, the company does not require increased investments to achieve its strategic goals – the network has sufficient capacity after past expansions, and focusing on efficiency means making better use of existing assets.

FCF in 2024 amounted to about $6.65 billion (+24% YoY). In 2025-2027, my model assumes the growth of CFOs to $9.7–10 billion with a stable CapEx of about $3.1-3.4 billion, which allows generating more than $7.5 billion FCF annually. This is enough to cover dividends, smoothly increase cashbacks, and maintain flexibility. At the same time, investments remain rational — the company does not spend excessively, but focuses on improving efficiency and profitability. Even with moderate revenue growth (3-4% per year) I expect the outstripping growth of free cash flow.

In 2024, the dividend per share was $5.28 with a yield of about 2.38%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $3.251 billion in dividends or $5.38 per share with a yield of 2.41%.

Valuation

The company's shares have fallen by 7.17% over the past year, and the main drop occurred in April 2025 after the news about the introduction of tariffs, the entire market fell sharply. At the same time, Union Pacific has lagged behind market growth over the past 12 months, as the S&P 500 has grown by 4.74%. I believe that after the April correction, the company's shares have gained an upside in price by 9-10%, which can be used.

The company is trading at a discount to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the discount on P/E is 17%, and on EV/EBITDA about 25%.

At the same time, compared with peers, the company is trading near average values in terms of P/E and EV/EBITDA multipliers, and at a premium in terms of P/B and P/S, as can be seen in the table below. It can also be noted that Union Pacific is the most profitable company among peers and does not lag behind the market average in terms of revenue growth.

In my forecast of the target price, I use four estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), target value of P/E, P/S and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 20%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 6.87%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 21.95x, P/S is 6.13x, and EV/EBITDA is 14.49x (took historical averages).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $245.06 with a growth potential of 9.63% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 12.04%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. Wall Street analysts are slightly less conservative in their price forecasts, but the general view of the company is similar – the consensus expects a price of $257.63 with an upside of about 15%.

Key risks

The most obvious risk is the very nature of the market: it is mature, overheated and growing very slowly, which calls into question the possibility of large—scale expansion. Against this background, the emergence of CPKC with fresh infrastructure and ambitions to take over north-south transportation — especially through Mexico — looks like a real challenge. Add to this competition from motor transport and you get a very aggressive environment. Trucks are more flexible, faster on short distances, and customers can easily swap a railroad for a truck if disruptions start somewhere. And failures are not that uncommon: the year 2022 showed that even giants like UP have serious reliability problems. In addition, the macro. A decline in industrial production, a new wave of trade wars, or a possible increase in Federal Reserve interest rates can all instantly hit cargo flows.

Key growth drivers

With the arrival of Jim Vienna, a serious reboot began: the focus is not on reducing for the sake of reporting, but on real growth and improving the quality of service. And the first results have already been achieved, especially in intermodal transport, which is returning to its previous volumes after the recession, especially in connection with the ports of the West Coast. The key bet on environmental friendliness is also starting to work: corporate clients are increasingly looking for “green” logistics, and in terms of emissions, UP looks very convincing. The company's network is generally a separate story. UP controls all major crossings with Mexico, and this gives it a huge advantage in a situation where cross-border trade is growing. Plus, strong financial indicators include an EBITDA margin of about 50% in 2024, high free cash flow, and a manageable debt burden. This gives the company the opportunity not to choose between investments, dividends and buybacks — it can do everything at once.

Conclusion

Union Pacific combines moderate revenue growth with improving margins, leading to faster profit growth. The company generates high free cash flow, which allows it to reduce debts, finance new initiatives, and direct money to cashback or dividends. Return on equity remains high (over 15% ROIC), reflecting the success of investments and barriers to entry in the industry. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $245.06 with a growth potential of 9.63% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 12.04%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Producer Price Index: Wholesale Inflation Unexpectedly Falls In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Wholesale inflation unexpectedly fell in March, dropping for the first time in 17 months. The producer price index for final demand was down 0.4% month-over-month after a 0.1% increase in February. This was lower than the expected 0.2% growth. On an annual basis, headline PPI increased 2.7%, down from 3.2% in February and below the 3.3% forecast.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy, fell 0.1% in March, down from 0.1% in February and lower than the expected 0.3% growth. This marks the first monthly decline since July. On an annual basis, core PPI eased to 3.3% from 3.5% in February and came in below the 3.6% forecast.

Producer Price Index: Finished Goods

The BLS shifted its focus to the "final demand" PPI series in 2014, but data for these series extend only back to November 2009 for headline PPI and April 2010 for core PPI. Since our analysis emphasizes longer-term trends, we continue to track the legacy PPI for finished goods, which the BLS still includes in its monthly updates. As a later overlay chart will illustrate, the final demand and finished goods indexes remain highly correlated.

In March, the PPI for finished goods fell 1.3% month-over-month, down from 0.2% in February. This is the largest monthly decline in 17 months. Year-over-year, headline PPI for finished goods slowed to 1.0% from 2.1% in February. Meanwhile, core PPI for finished goods was up 0.2% on the month, down from the 0.3% in February. On an annual basis, core PPI for finished goods was unchanged at 2.3%.

Producer Price Index (PPI) vs. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Both PPI and CPI illustrate monthly price changes. However, as their names suggest, the Producer Price Index measures price changes from the producer perspective whereas the Consumer Price Index measures price changes from the consumer perspective. PPI is thought to be a leading indicator of consumer inflation because, for the most part, when producers pay more for goods and services they are likely to pass along those higher costs to the consumer. With that being said, during the 2020 recession, producers were unable to pass along price increases, demonstrating the higher volatility of core PPI than core CPI. This relationship is further illustrated in the next chart.

For a closer look at the two main measures of inflation (CPI and PCE) and how they stack up against each other, check out here. Note: data is through January 2024.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Birkenstock: Upgrade To Buy On Cheaper Valuation
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Birkenstock (NYSE:BIRK) stock previously with a hold rating, as I did not find the upside attractive at that valuation. I have now changed my mind. Valuation has become a lot cheaper after the sell-down. The stock is trading at ~22x forward P/E as I write this update, and since fundamentals have not deteriorated, I am upgrading to a buy.

Resilient Top-Line Growth Across Channels and Geographies

Like I have expected, BIRK continues to report robust revenue growth rates, showing no signs of momentum slowdown. For the benefit of readers that did not catch up with the last earnings, I thought this would be helpful: 1Q25 revenue grew 19% y/y on a constant currency [CC] basis and is tracking above management’s FY25 CC revenue guidance of 15–17%. Importantly, like in 4Q24, the growth was across all regions—the Americas grew 16% y/y, EMEA was up 17% y/y, and APAC accelerated sharply to 47% y/y growth. By channel, B2B revenue grew 30% y/y, while DTC grew 10% y/y.

This performance itself is solid proof that BIRK is not seeing any form of demand slowdown. In fact, management’s tone and commentary during the call suggest that underlying demand remains very strong. Specifically, they noted “zero global demand destruction” and no order cancellations across their wholesale network. In fact, order books remain >20% above current supply, and that is across every region. If you consider that multiple headline news have indicated poor retail spending and a decline in discretionary spending, this suggests that BIRK is winning a ton of relative market share.

DTC Strategy Building Momentum into 2H

BIRK's growth outlook remains robust, in my view, with direct-to-consumer [DTC] being the main driver. While DTC growth came in at only 10% y/y for 1Q25, this figure should accelerate in the coming quarters, supported by both new store rollouts. BIRK ended 4Q24 with 71 stores, and management has plans to bring that total to ~100 by the end of FY25, which implies ~40% increase in footprint within a year. We know that underlying demand is strong, so if we assume revenue/store stays flat, this implies DTC revenue could grow by ~40% in FY25, which means the coming quarters growth has to accelerate meaningfully. The momentum at the digital front adds another layer to this DTC accelerating growth story. BIRK now has 8.8 million DTC members, up ~30% y/y, and these members spend ~30% more per transaction vs. non-members. We know that this membership strategy is clearly becoming a growth driver, as ~50% of DTC revenue comes from members.

What this tells me is that BIRK is enjoying a very powerful flywheel. Here is how I see it: more members mean higher transaction value, increased purchase frequency, and lower customer acquisition costs [CAC]. And what makes this even more compelling is that BIRK already has a very efficient customer acquisition strategy. This membership strategy would lower it by even more. Specifically, management noted that 90% of new customers are acquired through unpaid channels, with 60% of those coming via word-of-mouth. The combination of these means that BIRK is not only able to see better demand (higher revenue growth), it also improves customer lifetime value [CLTV] (i.e., better margins). And the beauty is that this entire cycle is a self-reinforcing loop that keeps getting better as BIRK scales.

Taken together, I think this sets up a really compelling case for DTC growth reacceleration.

The Pasewalk factory has reached an inflection point

The other notable margin driver here is BIRK's manufacturing footprint, particularly the ramp-up at its Pasewalk factory. In the call, management confirmed that utilization is now at an inflection point, and starting in 2Q25, they expect the facility to begin contributing positively to gross margins. The expectation is that by FY26, BIRK should recapture the full 150 bps of margin pressure seen in FY24 as the factory reaches a normalized utilization rate. While 150 bps may seem a little, remember that this is a business with relatively high fixed costs. A 150 bps expansion at the gross margin level equates to a sizable impact on the bottom line. Here is an illustration. If 1Q25 had that 150 bps of gross margin tailwind, gross profit would have increased by ~EUR5.4 million. Assuming fixed costs stay the same, this would translate into a ~7% increase in EBIT and a 10% increase in adjusted earnings. And if we compound that impact with the revenue growth strength over the next few quarters, the impact on earnings is likely to be much more.

Valuation

BIRK’s forward PE valuation is a lot more palatable than before. The stock is trading near its all-time low of 22x forward PE, a big discount from its average of 29x. Consensus estimates are also not incredibly tough to meet (~26% CAGR from FY24 to FY28) given growth momentum (FYI: 1Q25 EPS grew almost 100%) and margin expansion potential. Now, if we assume BIRK can deliver EUR3.1 adj EPS in FY28, and the stock doesn’t re-rate, the share price is worth ~EUR68, which represents a 42% upside. However, I think the more probable case is BIRK seeing at least a mean reversion as the market prices in the strong earnings growth potential. If multiple goes back to 29x, the upside is even more attractive.

Tariff Risks

The biggest risk is how BIRK would be impacted by the tariffs, particularly in the US. The indirect impact is that consumer demand may be significantly weakened as inflation spikes up, which impacts demand for BIRK goods. I cannot say for sure demand will not be impacted, but with Trump showing signs of reversing his sweeping tariff plans, this bear case may not materialize.

Conclusion

I upgrade my recommendation to a buy rating for BIRK. The recent derating has made the stock meaningfully more attractive. The business fundamentals remain solid, with DTC growth set to accelerate and margins to expand as Pasewalk ramps up. At ~22x forward P/E, I think the upside is attractive even if multiples do not re-rate higher.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/13/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: First Interstate BancSystem: Reasons To Remain Bearish Are Still There
data.description:
Back in May of last year, one company that I decided to downgrade was First Interstate BancSystem (NASDAQ:FIBK). I was turned off by its low asset quality and a decline in deposits that the bank was exhibiting. While shares were cheap compared to other similar firms and were attractive on a price to book basis, they weren't attractive relative to earnings. An overall decline in revenue and profits has recently weighed on the firm as well. Since downgrading the company from a ‘hold’ to a ‘sell’, the stock has fallen by 5.1%. That's almost double the 2.6% drop the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. As much as I would love to upgrade the company, given the most recent financial data available, that is just not in the cards. Those who want a firm that's cheap relative to its net assets, this might be a valid play. But for investors that want a quality enterprise, I would look elsewhere.

The picture remains disappointing

The most recent data that we have regarding First Interstate BancSystem covers the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. To start with, we should probably touch on its balance sheet. During this time, the enterprise had deposits of $23.02 billion. This actually represents a decline compared to the $23.32 billion in deposits the business ended 2023 with. However, it is up from the $22.81 billion it had on its books in the first quarter of 2024. That was the data that the business had out at the time that I last wrote about it. So in that respect, at least there has been some improvement. Year over year, the company's deposits dropped because of weakness across major categories like non-interest-bearing deposits, demand deposits, and time and other deposits. The good news is that the firm had no brokered deposits at the end of either year. The only increases in enjoyed came from savings deposits, which grew from $7.78 billion to $7.83 billion, and time deposits that had $250,000 or more in them. These time deposits expanded from $811.6 million to $825 million.

Loans are also an important part of the balance sheet that deserve attention. At the end of 2023, First Interstate BancSystem had $18.05 billion worth. This number fell to $17.64 billion by the end of last year. The largest chunk of its loan portfolio involves commercial real estate. About 52% of its loans fall under that category. This is followed up by commercial loans at 16% and residential real estate at 12%. The other categories of loans that the company has include construction real estate, agriculture, consumer, and agriculture real estate. I know that investors have been worried about exposure to office properties given the high vacancy rates that they have had over the last few years. The good news is that, if we exclude medical properties that have office space to them, only about 5.9% of the company's loans involve office assets.

Naturally, there are other aspects of the balance sheet we should touch on as well. For instance, at the end of 2024, First Interstate BancSystem had $7.74 billion invested in securities. This was down from the $9.05 billion reported the previous year. Over the same window of time, cash and cash equivalents did grow from $578 million to $896.6 million. Meanwhile, debt totaled $2.22 billion. What we can see by looking at the firm's data is that the decline in loans and securities was done at least in part in order to pay down this debt. That's because, back in 2023, First Interstate BancSystem had debt of $3.51 billion.

When it comes to the income statement, the picture has been disappointing in my opinion. For the final quarter of 2024, First Interstate BancSystem generated net interest income of $182.2 million. That's down from the $204.1 million reported the previous year. This was in spite of the fact that the company saw its net interest margin rise from 3.01% to 3.20%. Part of the problem was that the firm's provision for credit losses spiked from $5.4 million to $33.7 million. Furthermore, the value of its assets dropped from $30.67 billion to $29.14 billion. A larger decline in the value of liabilities still enabled its book value of equity to climb from $3.23 billion to $3.30 billion. But that was not enough to offset the other weaknesses I mentioned. For the year as a whole, net interest income contracted from $853.6 million to $760.4 million. For this time, the company did suffer a drop in its net interest margin from 3.14% to 3.04%. Meanwhile, the provision for credit losses jumped from $32.2 million to $67.8 million.

We have seen some strength when it comes to non-interest income. From 2023 to 2024, it expanded from $147 million to $178.1 million, with the final quarter of the year seeing an expansion from $44.5 million to $47 million. But this was largely made possible by the fact that, in 2023, the company booked a $23.5 million loss on investment securities. Had it not been for this, the rise in non-interest income would have been only $7.6 million. For the final quarter of 2024, net profits fell to $52.1 million from $61.5 million, with the profit for the year as a whole dropping from $257.5 million to $226 million.

I don't have a problem with a decline in revenue and profits if shares are cheap enough. But to me, they aren't. In the chart above, you can see that First Interstate BancSystem is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 11.9. This is above the 6 to 10 range that I typically like to see. However, this is not a ridiculous trading multiple compared to other similar businesses. In that same chart, you can see the firm stacked up against five such enterprises. Two of the five ended up being cheaper than it is on this basis. In the chart below, meanwhile, I decided to compare First Interstate BancSystem to the same five companies using both the price to book multiple and the price to tangible book multiple. On a price to book basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it comes down to the price to tangible book multiple, only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is.

These price to book multiples would generally be attractive to me. However, one thing that I have a problem with is that the asset quality of the business is lower than I would like it to be. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to the same five businesses. The 0.70% reading that we get here is below the 1% minimum that I typically prefer. Furthermore, four of the five competitors are higher than it is on this basis. And in the subsequent chart, you can see that when it comes to return on equity, the 6.22% that the business has, which is significantly below the 10% minimum I like to see, places it at the bottom of the group.

Takeaway

At this time, I see no reason to become optimistic about First Interstate BancSystem. If shares were cheaper, I might be more neutral on the business. The same would hold true if asset quality was high. But given where the company is, in addition to the broader economic uncertainty that we are now facing, I believe that maintaining a ‘sell’ rating is appropriate right now.

data.title: Toast: In-House R&D Makes The Business More Agile
data.description:
Toast (NYSE:TOST) looks overvalued from an absolute perspective and relative perspective. But leading companies with strong generation of FCF/EC, shareholder friendly policies, independent from other companies (Doesn’t acquire or depend on third parties for product innovation) and with considerable growth opportunities rarely are at fair value. I rate Toast stock a buy.

Background

Toast has been able to conquer the US market without any major acquisitions. A very hard feat for a company that generates most of its revenue via payments processing.

In fact, payment processing is a tough market because multiple banks and fintechs offer commoditized take rates. A large share of the payments market is related to businesses that wish to keep their operations simple while enjoying low costs.

Toast, with higher take rates, is able to consolidate part of the US market using software and equipment that provides advanced analytics and process automation. Moreover, Toast hasn’t expanded with major acquisitions like we typically see from its competitor Shift4 (FOUR).

Toast products were mainly developed in house. This company has a powerful R&D ability that is translating into strong products and a powerful brand.

Strategy

Sell to profitable restaurants a platform of vertically integrated solutions that produce automation and added value analytics. Toast focuses on profitable restaurants that don’t mind significant fixed costs and above market take rates. The company boasts under its portfolio over half of the US Michelin star-rated restaurants. This shows the power of Toast’s solution to deliver premium customer experiences with streamlined processes and added features. At the same time, Toast’s restaurants are faster at processing clients, increasing table turnover and thus revenue. The costs of the restaurants are also minimized through back-office automation.

In addition to automation, Toast’s clients have access to the largest dataset of SMB and mid-market restaurants, providing great benchmarking and valuable analytics in this volatile industry. The data advantage helps Toast’s restaurants to make better decisions in procurement, operations, employees’ salaries and restaurant menus, translating into better strategical and tactical decisions with more lean operations. Consequently, producing more revenue and lower costs.

Toast applies its solution with a large focus and detail, touching on all restaurant operations. Takeout, kitchen operations, ordering processes, and payment processes are streamlined, providing a very vertical solution for its restaurant merchants.

In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

From day one, our vertical strategy has served us well by focusing on solving the needs of restaurants better than anybody else in the world. This has allowed us to deepen our market share and establish a leadership position across SMB and mid-market restaurants here in the US.

Provide competitive rates relative to its product quality to take over the US market and thus build a data moat. Toast’s take rates should be more expensive than banks or other legacy fintechs. However, if we compare Toast’s package to other comparable packages like Shift4’s SkyTab, we can understand that Toast actually provides a very competitive solution. That’s also a reason why the company is increasing so fast and organically.

According to this link, Toast provides a take rate of 2.49% on the transacted amount, while Shift4’s SkyTab provides a take rate of 2.75%. Toast fixed costs should be higher than SkyTab. But for mid-market and SMB’s customers, that generate a lot of volume, TOST’s package is much more attractive. Toast should also be better for restaurants that need to make more strategic and tactical decisions due to better analytics, compared to SkyTab.

Financials

Toast produces much lower gross margins than Shift4. One of the reasons is explained previously. Toast’s take rates are inferior in the restaurant business, compared to Shift4’s. Shift4 is also more diversified into higher gross margins businesses like hotels and stadiums. Toast also provides its physical products below cost to win over the clients, affecting gross margins. Both companies’ gross margins have increased significantly in the past year.

Toast has a much more asset light business, with much less money invested in Goodwill and intangible assets. Despite generating much lower FCFexSBC, while spending almost 6 times Shift4’s SBC, its balance sheet is so light that it produces more FCFexSBC/EC than Shift4.

Shift4 is expected to grow faster in the next 3 years. The growth is fueled by acquisitions that will be constrained by debt levels. Toast should have lower growth, but is much higher quality because it is organic. If Toast is able to pivot to international markets with good execution, its growth estimates should go higher.

In the net debt department, Toast has 8% of the market cap in cash while Shift4 has 25% of the market cap in net debt

On the Price to FCFexSBC, Shift4 is massively undervalued in comparison to Toast. However, Toast is showing friendly shareholder policies. Indeed, the SBC is reducing as the company grows revenue. Thus, TOST’s high multiple should compress a lot in the future.

Opportunities

As mentioned before, Toast is generating a large data moat. Its focus on expanding the US market via lower relative take rates. Then, absorbing data from the restaurants to support strategic and tactical decision-making through decision support systems, was a very well executed strategy that is providing a lot of differentiation and brand recognition.

The automation and process improvement features are nice to have. But, for me, the segment that is taking market share in the premium restaurants is the advanced analytics product. That other companies like Shift4, at least from what I searched, is lacking.

In the latest earnings call, the company management said this:

This benchmarking tool has been a game-changer for many of our customers, including our team at Äta, a full-service restaurant in Louisville, who have used our insights to enhance their menu, add new specials, and even change their hours of operation to grow their business. For example, they noticed that chicken sales were underrepresented in restaurants near them by using this data, and used that to add and market a fried chicken special on a slower night that boosted Tuesday night revenues by 40%; that's the power of our data.

Toast, with this differentiated advantage, should take more market share in the US and abroad

Risks

Tariffs will overall affect restaurant consumption, including consumption in Toast’s merchants. Wealthy individuals are already losing massive amounts of money in the markets, which will affect how much they spend in restaurants.

The other potential risk is Shift4, which has more than 100 thousand restaurants in the US, developing an advanced analytics and benchmarking product that will reduce Toast’s differentiation.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. To be conservative, I assumed an initial FCF margin of 6%. Then it expands 0.5% every year. To generate the terminal multiple, I’m using a terminal growth rate of 4.7%. The terminal growth rate is obtained by summing a real GDP growth rate of 2.5% and an inflation rate (since 2000) of 2.2%, resulting in the number 4.7%. I also take into consideration the share count to calculate the FCF per share. It expands by 3% every year. With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current share price.

The model outputs an initial growth rate of 21.9% that reduces 5% every year, reaching 13.8% in 2034. Considering the sell-side analysts’ estimations for the next three years, Toast is somewhat overvalued. Despite this, Toast is a high-quality company, with potential for long-term growth through internationalization, entering new markets and increasing take rates. High-quality companies usually never trade on fair value. Thus, right now, Toast stock price looks attractive.

Conclusion

Toast has a much more R&D, built in-house, heavy business strategy that is showing results. I also like the fact that Toast’s take rates are lower than Shift4’s because it allows the former to take market share and build a data moat. Moreover, the company brand becomes more diffused by taking market share while building good products. Shift4 is also attractive because it is very undervalued and has been executing well its strategy of acquiring and expanding. Overall, I think Toast will outperform Shift4 in the future because of in-house R&D, which is much more agile at dealing with market changes and customer feedback. I like both companies from a buying point of view, but I prefer longing Toast over Shift4. I rate Toast a buy.
